answer_text: I would recommend the Hartmann Century Deluxe Carry-On for a stylish overpacker because it is presented as a “distinctive hardside” carry-on with a signature print, sophisticated colors, a garment sleeve, spacious packing compartment, zippered pockets, tie-down straps, and 1.5” expansion for a little extra room. [Hartmann] The travel-size limitation to keep in mind is that its listed overall dimensions are 22"H x 14"L x 9"W before relying on the expansion, and the page warns that carry-on size and weight allowances vary by airline. [Hartmann] If your trip-length priority matters more than style and expansion, Away is the clearest 3-5 day fit because its Carry-On is marketed for 3-5 day trips and 5-7 outfits, but it does not offer the same listed expansion feature. [Away]
overall_shopper_takeaway: Pick Hartmann for stylish expandable value, Briggs & Riley for the strongest traditional luggage-brand warranty and business organization, Hartmann again for the best lighter budget-conscious hard-shell balance, July for hard-shell protection and built-in tech features, and Away if you want the clearest 3-5 day sizing with compression and broad carry-on compatibility. [Hartmann] [Briggs & Riley] [July] [Away]

answer_text: The Briggs & Riley Baseline Essential 22" Carry-On Expandable Spinner seems best for a traditional luggage-brand choice with strong protection, smooth handling, and warranty reassurance. [Briggs & Riley] It stands out because it has impact-resistant corner guards, self-repairing YKK zippers, a TSA-accepted Travel Sentry combination cable lock, 360º spinner wheels for smooth silent movement, and an aerospace-grade aluminum multi-height handle. [Briggs & Riley] Its CX Smart Packing Technology expands up to 30% and then compresses back to carry-on size for most US overhead bins, which is especially useful for packing flexibility without immediately checking the bag. [Briggs & Riley] The strongest reassurance is the lifetime guarantee: the page says Briggs & Riley will repair the bag for life, free of charge, even if it is damaged by an airline, with no proof of purchase needed. [Briggs & Riley]

answer_text: The Hartmann Century Deluxe Carry-On seems like the best balance for a lighter, budget-conscious hard-shell carry-on with the requested basics. [Hartmann] It is priced at $300, weighs 7.3 lbs, uses polycarbonate, has double spinner wheels with ball bearings, includes a recessed TSA lock, and offers basic organization through a removable garment sleeve, ample zippered pockets, and nylon tie-down straps. [Hartmann] RIMOWA is much lighter at 4.9 lbs and also has a polycarbonate shell, TSA-approved locks, Multiwheel system, mesh divider, and zipped pocket, but its $700 price makes it less budget-conscious. [RIMOWA] Compared with higher-priced options, you may give up stronger warranty reassurance and more advanced protection or packing systems: Hartmann lists a limited 10-year global warranty, while RIMOWA, July, Away, and Briggs & Riley list lifetime coverage or lifetime warranty language, and Briggs & Riley adds CX expansion/compression plus airline-damage repair coverage. answer_text: The strongest protection-focused standouts are July and Briggs & Riley, but they protect the bag in different ways. [July] [Briggs & Riley] July is the stronger hard-shell protection pick because it uses an aerospace-grade German polycarbonate shell, reinforced bumpers, an integrated TSA lock, YKK Japanese zippers, and a curved eggshell design that the page says minimizes damage, with anodized aluminum bumpers for extra protection on hard drops. [July] Briggs & Riley is the stronger rough-handling and repair-reassurance pick because it uses premium ultra-durable ballistic nylon, impact-resistant corner guards, self-repairing YKK zippers, a Travel Sentry TSA-accepted cable lock, and a lifetime guarantee that covers airline damage repairs. [Briggs & Riley] Away also stands out for tested durability and zipper security: its page lists a rigorously tested 100% polycarbonate shell, ergonomic reverse-coil zippers for easy access and extra security, a TSA-approved lock, handle pull testing, wheel testing, zipper testing, and loaded drop testing at 10° F. [Away] RIMOWA has a polycarbonate shell, TSA-approved locks, KK metal zippers, a Multiwheel system, and a lifetime guarantee, but the page provides fewer specific reinforced-corner or rough-drop protection details than July, Briggs & Riley, or Away. [RIMOWA] Hartmann has durable 100% polycarbonate with metallic film and a recessed TSA lock, but the page provides less detail about reinforced corners, zipper security, or rough-handling testing. [Hartmann]
overall_shopper_takeaway: Pick Hartmann for stylish expandable value, Briggs & Riley for the strongest traditional luggage-brand warranty and business organization, Hartmann again for the best lighter budget-conscious hard-shell balance, July for hard-shell protection and built-in tech features, and Away if you want the clearest 3-5 day sizing with compression and broad carry-on compatibility. [Hartmann] [Briggs & Riley] [July] [Away]

answer_text: For practical business or tech-heavy travel, July seems most useful if built-in charging, tracking-oriented features, laundry separation, and smooth mobility are priorities. [July] It includes an ejectable USB-C battery that the page says can charge both a phone and a laptop, advertises CASESAFE TRACKING, has a hidden stain-proof laundry bag, SilentMove 360° spinner wheels, a 20-height multi-stop telescopic handle, Y-Strap compression, and water-resistant, stain-proof nylon lining. [July] The product page does not explain the tracking feature in detail, so the exact tracking capability is not fully provided. [July] If you need business-style quick access more than built-in battery and tracking, Briggs & Riley is a strong alternative because it has a SpeedThru pocket, PowerPocket for a battery pack and USB cord pass-through, a large front pocket, two mesh-lid zip pockets, an integrated garment carrier, and 360º spinner wheels. [Briggs & Riley] Hartmann is also useful for tech-light business trips because it offers an integrated USB port, garment sleeve, zippered pockets, tie-down straps, and double spinner wheels, but the page says the battery is not included and does not mention tracking. [Hartmann]
